meta.name: US Citizenship Rules Engine
meta.description: Total-function decision tree for determining U.S. citizenship. Every path terminates in a CITIZEN or NOT_CITIZEN outcome. Each node carries the controlling legal authority.
meta.disclaimer: Educational tool. Not legal advice. Citizenship determinations are fact-specific; consult a qualified immigration attorney.
legal_frontiers: The 14th Amendment 'subject to the jurisdiction' branch (node Q1) is the subject of Trump v. Barbara (cert. granted Dec 2025). Reflects settled law (Wong Kim Ark, Plyler v. Doe) as of as_of_date.

outcome_kinds.NOT_CITIZEN: Person is not a U.S. citizen (includes U.S. nationals, who are not citizens).

node_kinds.question: A node requiring an input answer; has typed 'answers' that route onward.
node_kinds.outcome: A terminal node with an 'outcome' of CITIZEN or NOT_CITIZEN.

Q0.explanation: This determines which citizenship rules apply. People born in the U.S. are generally citizens at birth under the 14th Amendment. People born abroad may be citizens through a parent.
Q0.citation: 14th Amendment; 8 U.S.C. § 1401; United States v. Wong Kim Ark, 169 U.S. 649 (1898)

answers.label: In the United States (50 states or D.C.)
In a U.S. territory or possession
Found in the U.S. — parents unknown

Q0_ABROAD.prompt: Was at least one of this person's parents a U.S. citizen at the time of birth?
Q0_ABROAD.explanation: A child born abroad can acquire U.S. citizenship through a citizen parent, but physical presence and transmission requirements must be met. If neither parent was a citizen, citizenship must come through naturalization or derivation through a parent who later naturalizes.

answers.label: Yes — both parents were U.S. citizens
Yes — one parent was a U.S. citizen
No — neither parent was a U.S. citizen (or status is unknown)

Q0_ABROAD_ONE.prompt: Were there any special circumstances around the birth?
Q0_ABROAD_ONE.explanation: Standard births abroad to one citizen parent follow the § 1401(g) physical-presence rules. Surrogacy and international adoption have separate legal frameworks.

answers.label: Born via surrogacy or assisted reproduction
Internationally adopted by a U.S. citizen parent

Q0_ABROAD_NONE.prompt: Was the person internationally adopted by a U.S. citizen parent?
Q0_ABROAD_NONE.explanation: When neither parent was a citizen at birth, citizenship through adoption may still be available under the Child Citizenship Act or the N-600K process. Without adoption, the person is not a citizen by birth and would need to qualify through naturalization or a parent's later naturalization.

answers.label: Yes — adopted by a U.S. citizen parent

Q1.prompt: Did either parent hold full diplomatic immunity under the VCDR at the time of the birth?
Q1.explanation: The 14th Amendment 'subject to the jurisdiction' clause excludes children of diplomatic agents with full immunity (VCDR Art. 31). It does NOT exclude children of undocumented immigrants, tourists, visa holders, LPRs, or consular staff with only functional immunity.
Q1.citation: 14th Amendment; 8 U.S.C. § 1401(a); 7 FAM 1110; VCDR Art. 31; Wong Kim Ark; Plyler v. Doe, 457 U.S. 202 (1982)

answers.label: No (neither parent held full diplomatic immunity)
Yes (at least one parent held full diplomatic immunity)

Q1b.prompt: Was the other parent (the one without full diplomatic immunity) a U.S. citizen?
Q1b.explanation: A U.S. citizen parent is by definition subject to U.S. jurisdiction. A child born in the U.S. to a citizen parent and a diplomat parent inherits jurisdiction from the citizen parent and acquires 14th Amendment citizenship.
Q1b.citation: 14th Amendment; 7 FAM 1116.1-3; Wong Kim Ark, 169 U.S. at 682

answers.label: Yes (the non-diplomat parent is a U.S. citizen)
No (the non-diplomat parent is a foreign national)

Q3.prompt: Which U.S. territory or possession was the person born in?
Q3.explanation: Each territory has its own statutory rule. American Samoa and outlying possessions produce U.S. nationals, not citizens.

Q4.prompt: Was the person born in Puerto Rico on or after January 13, 1941?

Q4b.prompt: Did the pre-1941 person acquire U.S. citizen status under the Jones Act, or reside in the U.S. on or after Jan 13, 1941?

Q5.prompt: Was at least one parent a U.S. citizen at the time of the Canal Zone birth?

Q6.prompt: At the time of birth in American Samoa or Swains Island, what was the citizenship status of the parents?
Q6.explanation: Birth in American Samoa or Swains Island ordinarily creates U.S. national status — not citizenship — under INA § 308. However, a child can still acquire U.S. citizenship at birth if citizenship is transmitted through a parent under the same rules that govern children born abroad. Where no parental-transmission rule applies, the person is a U.S. national who may later naturalize.
Q6.citation: 8 U.S.C. § 1408; 8 U.S.C. § 1401(c), (g); 8 U.S.C. § 1409; Tuaua v. United States, 788 F.3d 300 (D.C. Cir. 2015)

answers.label: Both parents were U.S. citizens at the time of birth
One parent was a U.S. citizen; the other was a U.S. national (not a citizen)
One parent was a U.S. citizen; the other was a foreign national
Neither parent was a U.S. citizen at birth (both nationals, or both foreign nationals)

Q7.prompt: Has the person (born in an outlying possession) since become a naturalized U.S. citizen?

Q8.prompt: Was the person found in the U.S. while under age 5, with unknown parentage?

Q8b.prompt: Was it shown before the person turned 21 that they were NOT born in the United States?
Q8b.explanation: The foundling citizenship presumption is rebuttable before age 21.

Q10.prompt: Were BOTH parents U.S. citizens at the time of birth?

Q11.prompt: Had at least one citizen parent ever resided in the U.S. or a possession before the birth (any duration)?

Q20.prompt: What is the person's date-of-birth era? (Controls which transmission rule applies.)
Q20.explanation: The law in force AT THE DATE OF BIRTH controls physical-presence transmission requirements. This is the central temporal fork.
Q20.citation: 7 FAM 1130 era chart; Act of 1855; Act of May 24, 1934; Nationality Act of 1940; INA 1952; IRCA 1986

Q25.prompt: For this pre-1934 birth, which parent is the U.S. citizen?
Q25.explanation: Pre-1934 law transmitted citizenship only through the father. The 1994 Technical Amendments added § 1401(h), retroactively allowing transmission through a citizen mother who had U.S. residence.

Q25f.prompt: Did the citizen father have prior U.S. residence before the birth?

Q25m.prompt: Did the citizen mother have prior U.S. residence before the birth?

Q21.prompt: Were the parents married to each other at the time of birth?

Q22.prompt: Which parent is the U.S. citizen?

Q23.prompt: Did the citizen parent meet the physical-presence transmission requirement for the applicable era?
Q23.explanation: Era standards: post-Nov 14, 1986 = 5 years total, 2 after age 14; Dec 24, 1952 - Nov 13, 1986 = 10 years total, 5 after age 14; 1934-1952 = Nationality Act of 1940 rules. Count all days physically in the U.S.

Q24ret.prompt: Was the person born before Oct 10, 1978 AND subject to a citizenship-retention requirement?
Q24ret.explanation: Pre-1978 law required the child to reside in the U.S. for 2 years between ages 14 and 28 to RETAIN citizenship. Pub. L. 95-432 repealed this prospectively (did not restore those already lost).

Q24r2.prompt: Did the person MEET the retention requirement (2 years U.S. residence between ages 14 and 28)?

Q30.prompt: For this out-of-wedlock birth, which parent is the U.S. citizen?
Q30.citation: 8 U.S.C. § 1409; Nguyen v. INS, 533 U.S. 53 (2001); Morales-Santana

Q31.prompt: Was the citizen mother physically present in the U.S. for 1 continuous year before the birth?

Q32.prompt: For the citizen father, are ALL four § 1409(a) requirements met? (1) blood relationship by clear and convincing evidence; (2) father was a citizen at birth; (3) father agreed in writing to support until age 18; (4) legitimation/written acknowledgment/adjudication before the child turned 18.

Q33.prompt: Did the citizen father meet the physical-presence requirement (5 years total, 2 after age 14)?

Q34.prompt: Were the parents married after the birth AND was the child legitimated under the law of their residence/domicile before age 18?

GO_NAT.prompt: The person did not acquire citizenship at birth. Begin the naturalization test. Is the person a Lawful Permanent Resident (green card holder)?
GO_NAT.explanation: Bridge node: all 'no acquisition at birth' paths converge here to test naturalization.

GO_NAT_NOLPR.prompt: Without lawful permanent residence, the standard naturalization path is not yet available. Does either narrow exception apply?
GO_NAT_NOLPR.explanation: Only two routes can lead to citizenship without first holding a green card: certain military service, and derivative citizenship through a U.S.-citizen parent. Every other current immigration status — a visa, a pending marriage petition, or no lawful status at all — still requires becoming an LPR first, so it does not change today's determination. (Current status alone can be reviewed separately using Check Immigration Status from the result page.)

answers.label: Active duty or veteran of the U.S. armed forces
Has a U.S.-citizen parent (potential derivative citizenship)

Q48.prompt: Is the person married to and living in marital union with a U.S. citizen (3-year pathway), versus the standard 5-year pathway?

Q42.prompt: Has the person met the required continuous residence as an LPR (5 years, or 3 years for the spousal pathway)?

Q43.prompt: Is continuous residence unbroken (no single absence over 1 year without an approved N-470)?

Q44.prompt: Was the person physically present in the U.S. for at least half the required period (30 months of 5 years, or 18 months of 3 years)?

Q45.prompt: Does the person have good moral character for the statutory period?

Q45b.prompt: Is the good-moral-character bar PERMANENT (murder, or an aggravated felony committed after Nov 29, 1990)?

Q46.prompt: Can the person meet the English and civics requirements, or qualify for a waiver (50/20, 55/15, or N-648 disability)?

Q47.prompt: Is the person willing to take the Oath of Allegiance (or an eligible modified oath)?

Q49.prompt: Has the person served honorably in the U.S. armed forces?
Q49.explanation: Military service is an alternate naturalization route that can waive the LPR requirement.

Q50.prompt: Was the service during a designated period of hostilities?

Q42m.prompt: Does the person hold LPR status and have at least 1 year of honorable service?

Q60br.prompt: Is the person a child of a U.S.-citizen parent (potential derivative citizenship)?

Q61.prompt: Is the child under 18, an LPR, residing in the U.S. in the legal and physical custody of the citizen parent?
Q61.explanation: All conditions of § 1431 must be met simultaneously for automatic citizenship.

answers.label: No (child abroad or not yet LPR)

Q63.prompt: Is the child currently under 18, abroad, and in the legal and physical custody of the U.S. citizen parent?
Q63.explanation: § 1433 (Form N-600K) allows a citizen parent to apply for a certificate of citizenship for a child residing abroad. All three conditions — under 18, abroad, in parent’s custody — must be satisfied when the application is filed.

answers.label: Yes (under 18, abroad, in citizen parent’s custody)

QADOPT_VISA.prompt: What type of immigrant visa was the child admitted on?
QADOPT_VISA.explanation: IR-3/IH-3 visas are issued when a full and final adoption was completed abroad and at least one parent witnessed the child. IR-4/IH-4 visas are issued when the child is coming to the U.S. to be adopted (or one married parent did not witness the child). IR-2 visas are family-based and require the adoption to have occurred before the child's 16th birthday with 2 years of joint legal custody and residence.

answers.label: IR-3 or IH-3 — full adoption completed abroad, at least one parent witnessed the child
IR-4 or IH-4 — coming to U.S. to be adopted (or adoption abroad not witnessed by one parent)
IR-2 — family-based petition (adopted before age 16, 2-year joint custody/residence with adoptive parent)

QADOPT_IR3_UNDER18.prompt: Was the child admitted to the United States as a Lawful Permanent Resident before their 18th birthday?
QADOPT_IR3_UNDER18.explanation: Automatic citizenship under INA § 1431 requires admission as an LPR before age 18. USCIS automatically sends a Certificate of Citizenship to the U.S. address for children under 14 admitted on IR-3 or IH-3 visas who meet all conditions.
QADOPT_IR3_UNDER18.citation: 8 U.S.C. § 1431(a)(1); Child Citizenship Act of 2000; USCIS Policy Manual Vol. 12, Part H, Ch. 3

answers.label: No (admitted at 18 or older — LPR only)

QADOPT_IR3_PARENT_CIT.prompt: Was the adoptive parent a U.S. citizen at the time of the child's admission to the United States?
QADOPT_IR3_PARENT_CIT.explanation: § 1431 requires that the citizen parent hold U.S. citizenship at the moment citizenship transfers to the child. For IR-3/IH-3 cases that moment is admission. If the parent naturalized after the child's admission, automatic citizenship did not attach at that time.

answers.label: Yes (parent was a U.S. citizen at time of admission)
No (parent was not yet a citizen at admission)

QADOPT_IR3_CUSTODY.prompt: Does the child reside in the United States in the legal and physical custody of the U.S. citizen adoptive parent?

QADOPT_IR4_UNDER18.prompt: Was the child admitted to the United States as a Lawful Permanent Resident before their 18th birthday?
QADOPT_IR4_UNDER18.explanation: Children admitted on IR-4 or IH-4 visas enter as LPRs. If admitted at 18 or older, automatic citizenship under § 1431 is unavailable regardless of whether the U.S. adoption is later finalized; the standard naturalization pathway applies instead.

answers.label: No (admitted at 18 or older — LPR only)

QADOPT_IR4_FINAL.prompt: Has the adoption been finalized in a U.S. state court before the child's 18th birthday?
QADOPT_IR4_FINAL.explanation: Children admitted on IR-4 or IH-4 visas enter as LPRs but do NOT automatically acquire citizenship on admission. Citizenship under § 1431 attaches automatically on the date the U.S. adoption is finalized — provided all other § 1431 conditions (LPR, under 18, legal and physical custody) are satisfied at that moment.
QADOPT_IR4_FINAL.citation: 8 U.S.C. § 1431; 8 C.F.R. § 320.2; USCIS Policy Manual Vol. 12, Part H, Ch. 2

answers.label: No (not yet finalized, or finalized at or after age 18)

QADOPT_IR4_PARENT_CIT.prompt: Was the adoptive parent a U.S. citizen at the time the adoption was finalized in the United States?
QADOPT_IR4_PARENT_CIT.explanation: For IR-4/IH-4 cases, citizenship under § 1431 attaches on the date of U.S. finalization. The parent must hold U.S. citizenship at that moment, not merely at some later date.

answers.label: Yes (parent was a U.S. citizen at time of U.S. finalization)
No (parent was not yet a citizen when the adoption was finalized)

QADOPT_IR4_CUSTODY.prompt: At the time the U.S. adoption was finalized, did the child reside in the legal and physical custody of the U.S. citizen adoptive parent?

answers.label: No (custody not established at time of finalization)

QADOPT_IR2_UNDER18.prompt: Was the child under 18 years old at the time of admission to the United States as an LPR?
QADOPT_IR2_UNDER18.explanation: IR-2 children admitted before their 18th birthday who reside in the U.S. in the legal and physical custody of their U.S. citizen parent(s) automatically acquire citizenship. Those admitted at 18 or older become LPRs and must pursue naturalization.

answers.label: No (18 or older at admission — LPR only)

QADOPT_IR2_ADOPT_AGE.prompt: Was the adoption finalized before the child's 16th birthday — or before the 18th birthday if a sibling was also adopted by the same parent(s)?
QADOPT_IR2_ADOPT_AGE.explanation: INA § 101(b)(1)(E) defines an adopted child for immigration purposes as one adopted before age 16 (or age 18 under the sibling exception of § 101(b)(1)(F)). If the adoption occurred after these cutoffs the person does not qualify as a 'child' under the INA and automatic citizenship under § 1431 does not apply.
QADOPT_IR2_ADOPT_AGE.citation: 8 U.S.C. §§ 101(b)(1)(E)–(F); USCIS Policy Manual Vol. 12, Part H

answers.label: Yes (adoption finalized within the applicable age cutoff)
No (adoption occurred after the applicable age cutoff)

QADOPT_IR2_PARENT_CIT.prompt: Was the adoptive parent a U.S. citizen at the time of the child's admission to the United States?
QADOPT_IR2_PARENT_CIT.explanation: § 1431 requires citizen-parent status at the moment citizenship would transfer. For IR-2 cases, that is the date of U.S. admission (provided the child is under 18 and all other conditions are met).

answers.label: Yes (parent was a U.S. citizen at time of admission)
No (parent was not yet a citizen at admission)

QADOPT_IR2_CUSTODY.prompt: Does the child reside in the United States in the legal and physical custody of the U.S. citizen parent(s)?

Q70.prompt: Did the person perform any expatriating act under 8 U.S.C. § 1481(a)?
Q70.explanation: Acquired citizenship can be lost only by a voluntary expatriating act performed with specific intent to relinquish. The seven acts: foreign naturalization, foreign oath of allegiance, foreign military service, foreign government employment, formal renunciation, wartime renunciation, and treason/bearing arms.
Q70.citation: 8 U.S.C. § 1481(a); Afroyim v. Rusk, 387 U.S. 253 (1967); Vance v. Terrazas, 444 U.S. 252 (1980)

answers.label: Foreign naturalization / oath / military / government post

Q77.prompt: Can the government prove the person performed the act with the SPECIFIC INTENT to relinquish U.S. citizenship?
Q77.explanation: Post-Afroyim/Vance, the burden is on the government, by preponderance of evidence. State Dept policy presumes retention absent an affirmative relinquishment statement.

Q78.prompt: Was the act voluntary, free of duress, with the citizenship consequences understood?

Q80reacq.prompt: Will the former citizen (who lost status for failing the retention requirement) take the oath to regain citizenship under § 1435(d)?

Q81marr.prompt: If citizenship was lost by marriage to an alien before Sept 22, 1922, does the person apply to re-acquire under § 1435?

CIT_TERR.title: U.S. Citizen — Guam / USVI / CNMI / AK / HI

CIT_CONF.title: U.S. Citizen — confirmed, no valid loss
CIT_CONF.citation: Afroyim v. Rusk; Vance v. Terrazas

NO_NATL.title: Not a U.S. Citizen — U.S. national only (§ 1408)

NO_NAT.title: Not a U.S. Citizen — naturalization requirements not met

NO_NAT_NOLPR.title: Not a U.S. Citizen — naturalization requires lawful permanent residence first

NO_PERM.title: Not a U.S. Citizen — permanently barred from naturalization

NO_LOST.title: Not a U.S. Citizen — citizenship lost (CLN)

NO_FOUND.title: Not a U.S. Citizen — foundling presumption rebutted

NO_PR.title: Not a U.S. Citizen — Puerto Rican citizen only (pre-1941)

root.prompt: Do you have a green card or Permanent Resident Card (Form I-551)?

q_lpr_type.prompt: What kind of resident card or resident filing is it?

answers.label: Two-year conditional card, or a pending/approved Form I-751 or I-829
Card has expired, but resident status continues
Filing only a replacement or renewal of the card
Resident card has been invalidated or revoked
Temporary worker (H, L, O, P, E, R)
Dependent of a principal nonimmigrant (H-4, L-2, F-2, etc.)

q_special_immigrant_type.prompt: Which special immigrant or related route best fits the person?

answers.label: Religious worker or other I-360 special immigrant

q_pending_type.prompt: What kind of case is pending?

answers.label: Nonimmigrant extension or change of status
Immigration court or other relief case

q_out_of_status_detail.prompt: Which description best fits the person's current situation?

answers.label: Status expired, with no pending filing
Not enough evidence to place status

q_review_path.prompt: Which evidence type is most likely to resolve the case?

status_lpr.title: Lawful Permanent Resident
status_lpr.definition: The person has permanent resident status.

status_cpr.title: Conditional Permanent Resident
status_cpr.definition: The person has conditional permanent residence.

status_refugee.title: Refugee
status_refugee.definition: The person was admitted as a refugee or has refugee derivative status.

status_asylee.title: Asylee
status_asylee.definition: The person has been granted asylum.

status_parolee.title: Parolee
status_parolee.definition: The person is in parole status.

status_daca.title: Deferred Action / DACA
status_daca.citation: DHS Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals policy (2012); 8 C.F.R. § 274a.12(c)(14)
status_daca.definition: The person has deferred action, including DACA.

status_student.title: Nonimmigrant Student
status_student.definition: The person has student nonimmigrant status.

status_worker.title: Nonimmigrant Worker
status_worker.definition: The person has temporary worker status.

status_fiance.title: Fiancé(e) Nonimmigrant
status_fiance.definition: The person holds a fiancé(e)-type nonimmigrant classification.

status_visitor.title: Visitor Nonimmigrant
status_visitor.definition: The person holds a visitor or business/tourist nonimmigrant classification.

status_dependent.title: Dependent Nonimmigrant
status_dependent.definition: The person is a dependent of a principal nonimmigrant.

status_nonimmigrant_other.title: Other Nonimmigrant
status_nonimmigrant_other.definition: The person has a nonimmigrant status that is not one of the common listed subtypes.

status_special_immigrant.title: Special Immigrant Category
status_special_immigrant.definition: The person fits a special-immigrant route such as SIJ or another I-360-based path.

status_u_nonimmigrant.title: U Nonimmigrant
status_u_nonimmigrant.definition: The person has U nonimmigrant status.

status_t_nonimmigrant.title: T Nonimmigrant
status_t_nonimmigrant.definition: The person has T nonimmigrant status.

status_humanitarian.title: Humanitarian Protection / Relief
status_humanitarian.definition: The person is in a humanitarian or protection-based posture such as VAWA or related relief.

status_national.title: U.S. National (Not Citizen)
status_national.definition: The person is a U.S. national, not a citizen.

status_pending_adjustment.title: Pending Adjustment
status_pending_adjustment.definition: The person has a pending I-485 or similar adjustment case.

status_pending_asylum.title: Pending Asylum
status_pending_asylum.definition: The person has a pending asylum case.

status_pending_tps.title: Pending TPS
status_pending_tps.definition: The person has a pending TPS case.

status_pending_daca.title: Pending DACA / Deferred Action
status_pending_daca.citation: DHS Deferred Action policy (2012); Form I-821D
status_pending_daca.definition: The person has a pending deferred-action or DACA request.

status_pending_nonimmigrant.title: Pending Nonimmigrant Extension / Change
status_pending_nonimmigrant.definition: The person has a pending nonimmigrant extension or change-of-status filing.

status_pending_humanitarian.title: Pending Humanitarian / Court Relief
status_pending_humanitarian.definition: The person has a pending humanitarian or immigration-court relief case.

status_pending_other.title: Pending / Unresolved
status_pending_other.definition: There is a pending or unresolved immigration filing, but it does not match a more specific category.

status_undocumented.title: Undocumented / Out of Status / Unresolved
status_undocumented.definition: The person appears to be without current lawful status.

status_review.title: Document / Record Review Needed
status_review.definition: The facts do not fit cleanly into a single status bucket and a document review is needed.

q_green_card_condition.prompt: Is it a 2-year conditional card, or do you have a pending or approved Form I-751 or I-829?

q_refugee_admission.prompt: Were you admitted as a refugee, or do you have refugee documents?

q_asylum_granted.prompt: Has asylum been granted to you?

q_parole.prompt: Are you in parole status or advance parole status?

q_tps.prompt: Do you have Temporary Protected Status (TPS)?

q_daca.prompt: Do you have deferred action or DACA?
q_daca.citation: DHS Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals policy (2012)

q_national.prompt: Does any official record say you are a U.S. national and not a U.S. citizen?

q_nonimmigrant_entry.prompt: Do you currently have a temporary visa or an I-94 with a nonimmigrant class?

q_special_or_humanitarian.prompt: Do you have any special immigrant or humanitarian paperwork, such as I-360, U visa, T visa, VAWA, or SIJ?

q_special_or_humanitarian_type.prompt: Which special or humanitarian route fits best?

answers.label: U visa (victim of a qualifying crime)

q_pending_case.prompt: Do you have any pending immigration case or receipt notice?

q_out_of_status.prompt: Which best describes the current situation?

answers.label: Status expired, with no pending case

Q1c.prompt: Did the diplomatic parent hold only less-than-full immunity (e.g., consular officer or administrative/technical staff)?
Q1c.explanation: Full diplomatic immunity under VCDR Art. 31 is reserved for diplomatic agents. Consular officers have only functional/official-acts immunity (VCCR Art. 43). Limited immunity does not break 14th Amendment jurisdiction.
Q1c.citation: 7 FAM 1112.1; VCDR Art. 31; VCCR Art. 43; Wong Kim Ark, 169 U.S. at 682

Q6A.prompt: Had at least one U.S. citizen parent resided in the United States or one of its outlying possessions before the birth (any duration)?
Q6A.explanation: When both parents are U.S. citizens, citizenship is transmitted to a child born in American Samoa or Swains Island if at least one parent previously resided in the U.S. or a U.S. possession. No minimum duration is required — any prior residence suffices.

answers.label: Yes (at least one citizen parent had prior U.S. or possession residence)
No (neither citizen parent had prior U.S. or possession residence)

Q6_ERA.prompt: What is the person's date-of-birth era? (Controls which physical-presence transmission requirement applies.)
Q6_ERA.explanation: The law in force at the date of birth controls how much physical presence the citizen parent needed to transmit citizenship. This mirrors the era analysis for children born abroad under § 1401.
Q6_ERA.citation: 7 FAM 1130 era chart; Act of May 24, 1934; Nationality Act of 1940; INA 1952; IRCA 1986

Q6_PRE34.prompt: For this pre-1934 birth in American Samoa or Swains Island, which parent is the U.S. citizen?
Q6_PRE34.explanation: Pre-1934 law transmitted citizenship only through the father. The 1994 Technical Amendments added § 1401(h), retroactively allowing transmission through a citizen mother who had U.S. residence before the birth.

Q6_PRE34F.prompt: Did the citizen father have prior U.S. residence before the birth?

Q6_PRE34M.prompt: Did the citizen mother have prior U.S. residence before the birth?

Q6_WED.prompt: Were the parents married to each other at the time of birth?
Q6_WED.explanation: Marital status determines whether the in-wedlock (§ 1401(g)) or out-of-wedlock (§ 1409) transmission rule applies. The § 1409 rules impose additional requirements — including acknowledgment and support — when the citizen parent is the father.

Q6_WHICH.prompt: Which parent is the U.S. citizen?

Q6_PP.prompt: Did the citizen parent meet the physical-presence transmission requirement for the applicable era?
Q6_PP.explanation: Era standards: post-Nov 14, 1986 = 5 years total, 2 after age 14; Dec 24, 1952–Nov 13, 1986 = 10 years total, 5 after age 14; 1934–1952 = Nationality Act of 1940 rules. Count all days physically present in the U.S.

Q6_OOW.prompt: For this out-of-wedlock birth in American Samoa or Swains Island, which parent is the U.S. citizen?
Q6_OOW.citation: 8 U.S.C. § 1409; Nguyen v. INS, 533 U.S. 53 (2001); Sessions v. Morales-Santana, 582 U.S. 47 (2017)

Q6_MOM_PP.prompt: Was the citizen mother physically present in the U.S. for 1 continuous year before the birth?

Q6_DAD_409.prompt: For the citizen father, are ALL four § 1409(a) requirements met? (1) blood relationship established by clear and convincing evidence; (2) father was a U.S. citizen at the time of birth; (3) father agreed in writing to financially support the child until age 18; (4) legitimation, written acknowledgment, or court adjudication of paternity before the child turned 18.

Q6_DAD_PP.prompt: Did the citizen father meet the physical-presence requirement (5 years total, 2 after age 14)?

Q6_LEG.prompt: Were the parents married after the birth AND was the child legitimated under the law of their residence or domicile before age 18?

Q6_NAT.prompt: Has the person (born in American Samoa or Swains Island, with no citizenship transmitted at birth) since become a naturalized U.S. citizen?
Q6_NAT.explanation: Where no parental-transmission rule applies, the person is a U.S. national — not a citizen — at birth. U.S. nationals may generally naturalize under more streamlined procedures than foreign nationals because they already owe permanent allegiance to the United States.
Q6_NAT.citation: 8 U.S.C. § 1408; 8 U.S.C. §§ 1421–1448; Tuaua v. United States, 788 F.3d 300 (D.C. Cir. 2015)

answers.label: No (remains a U.S. national, not a citizen)

Q63_PP.prompt: Does the citizen parent meet the physical-presence requirement of § 1401(g) — 5 years in the U.S. before the child’s birth, at least 2 of which were after the parent turned 14?

Q63_PRES.prompt: Was the child lawfully present in the United States at the time of the § 1433 interview?
Q63_PRES.explanation: USCIS requires the child to be physically and lawfully present in the U.S. at the time of the adjudication interview for a § 1433 / N-600K application to be approved.
Q63_PRES.citation: 8 U.S.C. § 1433(b); 8 C.F.R. § 322.3(a); USCIS Policy Manual Vol. 12, Part L

QSURR.prompt: Does the child have a genetic (biological) connection to a U.S. citizen parent?
QSURR.explanation: For children conceived via assisted reproductive technology (ART) and born via surrogacy, the State Department applies 7 FAM 1131.4-3: citizenship at birth requires a genetic link to a citizen parent. A child with no genetic connection to any citizen parent does not acquire citizenship at birth through transmission — an adoption-based path may still be available.

answers.label: Yes — genetically connected to BOTH citizen parents
Yes — genetically connected to ONE citizen parent
No genetic connection to any citizen parent

QSURR_ADOPT.prompt: Has the child been legally adopted by a U.S. citizen parent?
QSURR_ADOPT.explanation: Where no genetic connection exists, citizenship at birth through transmission is unavailable. However, a legal adoption may still open derivative citizenship or naturalization paths.
QSURR_ADOPT.citation: 7 FAM 1131.4-3; 8 U.S.C. §§ 1431, 1433; USCIS Policy Manual Vol. 12, Part H

answers.label: Yes (legally adopted by a U.S. citizen parent)

Q_DENAT.prompt: Has the person’s naturalization been revoked by a federal court under § 1451 (denaturalization)?
Q_DENAT.explanation: Denaturalization is a civil or criminal court proceeding to revoke a certificate of naturalization obtained by fraud, willful misrepresentation, concealment of a material fact, or illegal procurement. A revocation strips citizenship retroactively.
Q_DENAT.citation: 8 U.S.C. § 1451; Luria v. United States, 231 U.S. 9 (1913); United States v. Maslenjak, 582 U.S. 357 (2017)

Q_REN_FORM.prompt: Was the renunciation executed before a U.S. diplomatic or consular officer abroad (§ 1481(a)(5)), or before a designated official during a declared state of war (§ 1481(a)(6))?
Q_REN_FORM.explanation: A purported renunciation of U.S. citizenship is legally effective only if performed in the prescribed form. § 1481(a)(5) requires an oath before a U.S. diplomatic or consular officer outside the United States. § 1481(a)(6) allows renunciation before a designated official in wartime, in the U.S., with prior AG approval. An informal statement or oath before a foreign official has no legal effect.
Q_REN_FORM.citation: 8 U.S.C. § 1481(a)(5)–(6); 7 FAM 1260; Matter of Nolan, 19 I. & N. Dec. 674 (BIA 1988)

answers.label: No — form not satisfied (no valid renunciation)

Q_DER_ERA.prompt: Did the qualifying event (parent’s naturalization, or child’s admission as LPR) occur on or after February 27, 2001?
Q_DER_ERA.explanation: The Child Citizenship Act of 2000 (CCA) took effect February 27, 2001 and replaced former INA § 321 / 8 U.S.C. § 1432. For events on or after that date, § 1431 (CCA) governs. For events before that date, the repealed § 1432 may still apply.
Q_DER_ERA.citation: Child Citizenship Act of 2000, Pub. L. 106-395; former 8 U.S.C. § 1432 (repealed Feb. 27, 2001); 8 U.S.C. § 1431

Q_321.prompt: At the time of the parent’s naturalization, were the child’s parents married to each other?
Q_321.explanation: Under former INA § 321 (8 U.S.C. § 1432, repealed 2001), a child’s derivative citizenship depended on whether both parents naturalized (if married) or whether the single surviving or legally sole-custody parent naturalized.
Q_321.citation: Former 8 U.S.C. § 1432(a); Matter of Cross, 26 I. & N. Dec. 485 (BIA 2015)

answers.label: Yes — parents were married to each other
No — parents were divorced, separated, or one had died

Q_321_BOTH.prompt: Did BOTH parents naturalize as U.S. citizens?
Q_321_BOTH.explanation: Under former § 1432(a)(1), derivative citizenship attached when BOTH parents naturalized. If only one parent naturalized while the parents remained married, derivative citizenship did not attach under § 1432(a)(1).

answers.label: No — only one parent naturalized while both alive and married

Q_321_SINGLE.prompt: Did the custodial parent (or the surviving parent, if one had died) naturalize as a U.S. citizen while the child was under 18?
Q_321_SINGLE.explanation: Under former § 1432(a)(2)–(3), a child could derive citizenship through one naturalizing parent if: (a) the other parent was deceased; or (b) the naturalizing parent was the sole legal and physical custodian after a legal separation.
Q_321_SINGLE.citation: Former 8 U.S.C. § 1432(a)(2)–(3); Wedgewood v. INS, 217 F.3d 1278 (10th Cir. 2000)

answers.label: Yes — qualifying single parent naturalized while child was under 18

Q_321_LPR.prompt: Was the child a lawful permanent resident (LPR) residing in the United States at the time of the parent’s naturalization?
Q_321_LPR.explanation: Under former § 1432(a), the child had to be an LPR residing in the U.S. when the parent(s) naturalized. Temporary admission was insufficient. Residence after the parent’s naturalization did not cure the defect.
Q_321_LPR.citation: Former 8 U.S.C. § 1432(a); Matter of Nzomo, 25 I. & N. Dec. 285 (BIA 2010)

answers.label: Yes — child was an LPR residing in the U.S. at time of naturalization

CIT_DER321.title: U.S. Citizen — derivative citizenship under former INA § 321 / § 1432
CIT_DER321.citation: Former 8 U.S.C. § 1432 (repealed Feb. 27, 2001); Child Citizenship Act of 2000

NO_DENAT.title: Not a U.S. Citizen — naturalization revoked (denaturalized)
NO_DENAT.citation: 8 U.S.C. § 1451; Luria v. United States, 231 U.S. 9 (1913)
